critique.summary: The article critiques the modern obsession with collaboration in organizations, arguing that it often leads to diffusion of responsibility and reduced individual accountability. It highlights historical and organizational research showing that a small fraction of individuals do most of the work, while the rest provide minimal effort, and suggests that true ownership and responsibility are essential for productivity.
mainPoints: Historical research by S.L.A. Marshall found that only 15-20% of soldiers in combat actively engaged, illustrating diffusion of responsibility in groups.
The 80/20 principle recurs in organizational settings, where a minority of people do most of the work.
Modern tech tools aimed at enhancing collaboration often create excessive coordination overhead without increasing actual output.
Collaboration can obscure individual accountability, making failure a collective issue rather than an individual one.
Research by Ringelmann and Brooks shows that group size and added personnel can reduce individual effort and increase communication overhead.
High-quality work is often done by individuals or small groups with clear authority and accountability, not large collaborative teams.
The culture of collaboration can discourage decisive individual action and ownership, which are necessary to complete work effectively.
The article calls for a return to trusting individuals with responsibility and accountability rather than relying on extensive collaborative processes.
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately references well-documented phenomena such as the diffusion of responsibility, the Ringelmann effect, and Brooks' Law, all supported by historical and organizational research. The claim that collaboration tools can increase coordination overhead is consistent with studies on workplace productivity. However, the article presents a strongly opinionated critique without citing multiple sources or perspectives, and it generalizes about collaboration culture in a way that may not apply universally. The historical references to Marshall's combat research and IBM's feature usage are broadly accurate but simplified. Overall, the factual claims are grounded in recognized research, but the interpretation is subjective and somewhat one-sided.
considerations: The article does not consider the benefits of collaboration in fostering innovation, knowledge sharing, and employee engagement.
It overlooks contexts where collaboration is essential, such as complex problem-solving and creative industries.
The critique may not apply equally across all types of organizations or teams, especially those that successfully balance collaboration and individual accountability.
There is no discussion of how to effectively integrate collaboration and ownership rather than framing them as mutually exclusive.
The article does not address potential biases in the original studies cited, such as Marshall's methodology controversies.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the effectiveness and pitfalls of collaboration in work environments, with various commenters sharing personal experiences and insights. Many express skepticism about the value of collaboration, citing issues like poor management and the dilution of individual accountability.

comments.author: nuancebydefault
comments.text: It is true that on an individual level you get more work done if you collaborate less. But very often you will have solved the wrong problem. Collaboration often prevents that from happening because different point of views will make that clear before trying to solve a problem.
comments.analysis: This comment highlights a key tension in collaboration, recognizing both its potential benefits and drawbacks in problem-solving.

comments.author: cmenge
comments.text:
Read a super interesting paper by McEntire lately, “The Organizational Physics of Multi-Agent AI”.

In short, he proved that even AI agents exhibit all the dysfunctions one would normally attribute to human shortcomings / politics / laziness etc.

Either way, I think the point is strong: if the organization is bad, you end up doing mostly work about work which is exhausting.

Small, effective teams with super high accountability are more fun, but don't look "reproducible" or "repeatable".

Shameless plug on my take: https://www.menge.io/blog/where-to-cut/
comments.analysis: The commenter effectively connects organizational dysfunction to the inefficiencies of collaboration, providing a clear perspective on the issue.

comments.author: cgio
comments.text: What it misses is that the 80% of soldiers who were not firing was still required. Not everyone has the same product, and someone’s product exists at an abstraction layer above the outcome and towards the organisation that builds it, as ugly and inefficient as it may be judged in comparison to an army of perfect contributors that does not exist.
comments.analysis: This comment offers a nuanced view on the necessity of all roles in a collaborative effort, emphasizing the importance of every contribution.

comments.author: Ozzie_osman
comments.text: I think the author is overfitting. Collaboration and ownership are actually not in tension. _Bad process_ and ownership definitely can be. You can still have a high performance, high accountability culture that is collaborative.
comments.analysis: The comment presents a balanced perspective, suggesting that the issues with collaboration often stem from poor processes rather than collaboration itself.

comments.author: vielite1310
comments.text:
I think the Author might have a lot of bad collaboration experience from working with teams that have low level of competence and agency, and especially in corporate, this highlights and accidentally resonates with me ( as of few months ago)

Laid off from a startup and moved fo corpos did gave me perspective,the first year working with the team works really well, we managed to get a lot of stuff really done and business were very happy.

And there came the Agile Coaches telling us to "Collaborate" while disguising as a need to serve his own agenda ( as he's also a PO for another squad ). So workshops on Collaboration, Explicit Expectation on PM have all authority and controls PO, for 8 freaking months just to get a competent team to work with a junior team with no agency nor even willingness to be mentored or do anything. So somewhat this incidentally aligns perfectly.

Corporate always manage to hire incompetent people, not firing them, and let others over-compensate for their failures, so yeah, its not really obvious but its there.

I believe the good collaboration can happen, but when people actually go of their ego and start listening and actually doing the work.
comments.analysis: This comment reflects personal experience and suggests that the quality of collaboration is heavily influenced by team competence.

comments.author: jimnotgym
comments.text:
It is interesting to pick on an example like the Battle of the Bulge. To put those men, on both sides, in the field was an enormous effort of collaboration. We can say it was doomed from the beginning, in hindsight, but it was very dangerous at the time and took enormous efforts to disengage troops and redeploy them. Patton's redeployment must be one of the greatest organisational feats in history.

At the beginning of the Battle the weather was terrible, stopping the normal collaboration with the air force. When the weather cleared, collaboration restarted, and both arms could work together much more effectively than the army alone.
comments.analysis: The commenter draws an interesting historical parallel, illustrating the complexity of collaboration in high-stakes situations.

comments.author: sgbeal
comments.text: A tip for the author, if they're here: those big flashing cursors at the top and bottom of the page make it exceedingly difficult for some of us to read the nearby text. Human eyes have evolved to follow the flashiest thing around, so are continually pulled towards those flashes while trying to read. Mine struggle badly with the top and bottom sections, where those cursors are in frame, to the extent that i can't be bothered to do so.
comments.analysis: While this comment provides useful feedback, it diverges from the main discussion about collaboration and lacks depth.

comments.author: tqi
comments.text:
> But there’s a huge difference between communication and collaboration as infrastructure to support individual, high-agency ownership, and communication and collaboration as the primary activity of an organisation.

that is a meaningless buzzword salad masquerading as a deep insight
comments.analysis: This comment dismisses another's point without providing substantial reasoning, reflecting a more combative stance.
